content.explanation:
HTML provides six levels of headings, from <h1> (the most important) to <h6> (the least important). Headings define the hierarchical structure of your content, much like an outline in a book with chapters, sections, and subsections. They are block-level elements, meaning each heading starts on a new line and takes the full available width.

The <h1> element represents the main heading of the page and should typically appear only once per page. It tells both users and search engines what the page is primarily about. The <h2> elements represent major sections under the main topic, <h3> elements are subsections within an <h2>, and so on. You should not skip heading levels - going from <h1> directly to <h3> without an <h2> in between breaks the logical document outline and can confuse screen readers.

Headings play a critical role in Search Engine Optimization (SEO). Search engines like Google use headings to understand the content and structure of your page. The <h1> carries the most weight for ranking. Including relevant keywords naturally in your headings can improve your page's visibility in search results. However, stuffing keywords or using multiple <h1> tags is considered bad practice and can actually hurt your rankings.

Accessibility is another major reason to use headings correctly. Screen reader users often navigate pages by jumping between headings - it is one of the most common navigation strategies for people with visual impairments. The JAWS screen reader, for example, lets users press the H key to jump to the next heading, or press a specific number (1-6) to jump to headings of that level. If your headings are out of order or used only for visual sizing, this navigation breaks down.

Browsers apply default styles to headings - <h1> is the largest and boldest, decreasing in size through <h6>. However, you should never choose a heading level based on its default visual size. Always choose the semantically correct level for your content hierarchy, then use CSS to style the heading to the visual size you want. A common mistake is using <h3> everywhere because you like its default size - instead, use the correct heading level and apply CSS: h3 { font-size: 24px; }.

codeExamples.title: Heading Levels h1 through h6
codeExamples.output: Six headings displayed in decreasing size from h1 (largest) to h6 (smallest).
codeExamples.explanation: Each heading level has a default size and boldness. h1 is the largest and most prominent, while h6 is the smallest. Browsers apply these default styles automatically.

codeExamples.title: Proper Heading Hierarchy
codeExamples.code:
Web Development Guide

1. HTML Basics

1.1 What is HTML?

1.2 Document Structure

2. CSS Fundamentals

2.1 Selectors

2.2 Box Model

2.2.1 Margin

2.2.2 Padding

3. JavaScript

3.1 Variables

3.2 Functions
codeExamples.output: A structured document outline resembling a table of contents with chapters and sub-chapters.
codeExamples.explanation: Headings create a logical outline. Each h2 is a major section under the h1 topic. Each h3 is a subsection within its parent h2. This hierarchy helps screen readers and search engines understand content structure.

codeExamples.title: Common Heading Mistakes
codeExamples.code:
My Blog

First Post

This is not really a heading, I just wanted small bold text

First Post

Small bold text without using a heading

Main Title

Another Main Title

Main Title

Secondary Title
codeExamples.explanation: Never skip heading levels, use headings just for visual styling, or have multiple h1 tags. Choose the semantically correct level and use CSS for visual adjustments.

notes: Use only one <h1> per page. It should describe the main topic of the page.
Do not skip heading levels. Go from h1 to h2 to h3 in order. Skipping levels (h1 to h3) breaks the document outline.
Choose heading levels for semantic meaning, not visual size. Use CSS to adjust the appearance of headings.
Screen reader users navigate by headings. Proper heading hierarchy is essential for accessibility.
commonPitfalls: Using heading tags just to make text big and bold. Headings carry semantic meaning and should only be used for actual section headings. Use CSS for visual styling.
Having multiple h1 elements on a single page. While HTML5 technically allows this in sectioning content, it is best practice and better for SEO to have only one h1.
Skipping heading levels, such as jumping from h2 to h5. This confuses screen readers and breaks the logical document outline.
interviewTips: Know that h1-h6 elements are block-level, semantic heading elements that create a document outline.
Be prepared to explain why heading hierarchy matters for SEO and accessibility.

exercises.title: Create a Document Outline
exercises.description: Create a heading structure for a cooking recipe page. Use h1 for the recipe name 'Chocolate Chip Cookies', h2 for two sections: 'Ingredients' and 'Instructions', and h3 for sub-sections under Instructions: 'Preparation' and 'Baking'.
exercises.starterCode: <!-- Create the heading structure for a recipe page -->
hints: The recipe name is the main topic, so it gets h1
Ingredients and Instructions are major sections (h2), and Preparation/Baking are subsections of Instructions (h3)

exercises.title: Fix Heading Hierarchy
exercises.description: The following HTML has incorrect heading usage. Fix the heading levels so they follow a proper hierarchy without skipping levels. The page is about 'Travel Blog' with a section 'Europe Trip' containing sub-topics 'Paris' and 'Rome'.
hints: The main page topic should be h1, not h3
Sub-topics should follow sequentially: h1 > h2 > h3 without skipping levels

content.explanation:
The

element is the workhorse of HTML text content. It represents a paragraph - a block of related text separated from surrounding content by vertical space. Paragraphs are block-level elements, meaning each

starts on a new line and has default margin above and below it (typically around 16px in most browsers). You should wrap every distinct unit of text content in a

tag rather than leaving loose text directly in the body. The
element creates a line break within text content. It is a void element (no closing tag required). Use
when you need a line break that is part of the content itself - like in a poem, a mailing address, or song lyrics - not for adding space between paragraphs. A common mistake is using multiple

tags to add spacing between sections. This is incorrect; use CSS margin or padding for spacing, and use separate

elements for separate paragraphs. The

element creates a horizontal rule - a thematic break between sections of content. It renders as a horizontal line by default. In HTML5,
is not just decorative; it represents a semantic shift in topic. For example, you might use
between different scenes in a story or between different topics in an article. It is a void element and requires no closing tag. The default styling can be customized with CSS - you can change the color, height, border style, and width. The element (Word Break Opportunity) suggests a point where the browser may optionally break a long word if it would otherwise overflow its container. This is useful for very long strings like URLs, file paths, or technical identifiers that might not fit on one line. Unlike
, which always creates a break, only breaks the line if necessary. For example, in a narrow sidebar, a long URL like https://www.example.com/very/long/path might overflow; inserting after each slash allows the browser to break gracefully. Whitespace collapsing is essential to understand when working with text in HTML. The browser treats consecutive whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, newlines) as a single space. This means you can format your HTML source code with indentation and line breaks for readability without affecting the rendered output. If you need to preserve exact whitespace formatting, use the 
 element or the CSS property white-space: pre-wrap.

codeExamples.title: Paragraphs and Spacing
codeExamples.code:
The first paragraph discusses the introduction to our topic. Notice that the line break in the source code does not create a line break in the browser.

The second paragraph covers a different point. Browsers add vertical spacing between paragraphs automatically.

The third paragraph wraps up the discussion.
codeExamples.output: Three paragraphs displayed as separate blocks with vertical spacing between them.
codeExamples.explanation:
Each

element is a separate block with default margins. Line breaks in the HTML source are collapsed into single spaces in the rendered output. Separate ideas should be in separate paragraphs.

codeExamples.title: Line Breaks vs Paragraphs
codeExamples.code:
123 Main Street
Apartment 4B
New York, NY 10001

Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
HTML is fun,
And so are you.

First section

Second section
codeExamples.output: An address and a poem with proper line breaks, then two paragraphs with custom spacing.
codeExamples.explanation:
Use
for line breaks that are part of the content (addresses, poems). Use CSS margins for spacing between sections - never multiple
tags.

codeExamples.title: Horizontal Rules and Word Breaks
codeExamples.code:
Chapter 1: The Beginning

The story begins on a dark and stormy night...

Chapter 2: The Journey

Our hero sets out on an adventure...

Visit our documentation at: https://www.example.com/docs/getting-started/installation

File path: C:\Users\Documents\Projects\MyApp\src\index.html
codeExamples.output: Two chapter sections separated by a horizontal line, followed by a URL and file path that can break at marked points.
codeExamples.explanation: The <hr> element represents a thematic shift between content sections. The <wbr> element tells the browser where it is acceptable to break a long word or URL if needed to prevent overflow.

notes:
Always wrap text content in

tags. Leaving text directly in the without a container element is bad practice.

Use
only for line breaks that are part of the content (addresses, poetry). Never use multiple
tags for spacing - use CSS instead.
The <hr> element represents a thematic break, not just a visual line. Use it between sections of different topics.
The <wbr> element only creates a break when necessary. It is especially useful for long URLs and file paths in narrow containers.
commonPitfalls:
Using multiple
tags to create space between elements. This is a layout concern and should be handled with CSS margin or padding.
Forgetting that whitespace in HTML source code is collapsed. Writing extra spaces or line breaks in your code will not produce extra space in the rendered page.

Not wrapping text in

tags. Bare text in the is valid but semantically poor - it lacks structure and is harder to style.

content.explanation:
HTML provides a rich set of inline elements for formatting text. These elements are not just visual - they carry semantic meaning that helps browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies understand the importance and purpose of the text. Understanding the difference between semantic and purely visual formatting is key to writing good HTML.

The <strong> element indicates text of strong importance. Browsers render it as bold by default. It is different from the older <b> tag: <strong> means 'this text is important,' while <b> means 'this text should be visually bold without extra importance.' For example, a warning message should use <strong> because the content is genuinely important, while a product name in a review might use <b> because it is just visually distinguished. Similarly, <em> indicates emphasis (rendered as italic), while <i> is purely visual italics without emphasis.

The <mark> element highlights text as if it were marked with a yellow highlighter. It is commonly used to indicate search result matches or to draw attention to relevant text within a quoted passage. The <small> element represents side comments and small print, like legal disclaimers, copyright notices, or caveats. Despite its name, <small> is semantic - it means 'this is supplementary or legal text,' not just 'make this smaller.'

Subscript (<sub>) and superscript (<sup>) elements are used for text that should appear below or above the normal text baseline, respectively. Subscript is commonly used in chemical formulas (H<sub>2</sub>O) and mathematical variables. Superscript is used for footnote references, mathematical exponents (x<sup>2</sup>), and ordinal indicators (1<sup>st</sup>, 2<sup>nd</sup>). These elements are essential for correctly displaying scientific and mathematical notation.

The <del> and <ins> elements represent deleted and inserted text, respectively. <del> renders with a strikethrough line, indicating content that has been removed. <ins> renders with an underline, indicating content that has been added. These are particularly useful for showing document revisions, track changes, or price changes on e-commerce sites. Both elements accept datetime and cite attributes to indicate when and why the change was made.

codeExamples.title: Strong and Emphasis
codeExamples.code:
Warning: Do not delete the system files.

You must complete the form before submitting.

Critical: Back up your data immediately.

Product: SuperWidget Pro

Danger: High voltage
codeExamples.output: Warning text in bold, emphasized word in italics, critically important text in bold italics, and a product name in bold.
codeExamples.explanation: Use <strong> when text is genuinely important and <em> for emphasis that changes the meaning of a sentence. <b> and <i> are visual-only variants without semantic weight.

codeExamples.title: Mark, Small, and Highlighting
codeExamples.code:
Search results for "HTML":

Learn HTML basics in this comprehensive guide to HTML elements.

Premium Plan: $29/month

Prices may vary by region. Cancel anytime.

Sale: All items 50% off (while supplies last)
codeExamples.output: Search text with highlighted matches, pricing with fine print, and a sale announcement.
codeExamples.explanation: The <mark> element highlights text with a yellow background by default. <small> is used for supplementary information like disclaimers and fine print.

codeExamples.title: Subscript and Superscript
codeExamples.code:
Water: H2O

Carbon dioxide: CO2

Caffeine: C8H10N4O2

x2 + y2 = z2

210 = 1024

This claim has been verified.[1]

1st place, 2nd place, 3rd place
codeExamples.output: Chemical formulas with subscripts, math expressions with superscripts, a footnote reference, and ordinal numbers.
codeExamples.explanation: Subscript (<sub>) places text below the baseline for chemical formulas and variables. Superscript (<sup>) places text above for exponents, footnotes, and ordinals.

codeExamples.title: Deleted and Inserted Text
codeExamples.code:
Price: $49.99 $29.99 (Limited time offer)

The meeting is on Tuesday Wednesday at 3pm.

  • Set up project repository
  • Design database schema
  • Build API endpoints
  • Write unit tests
codeExamples.output: A crossed-out old price with new price, a schedule change, and a todo list with completed items struck through.
codeExamples.explanation: The <del> element shows removed content with a strikethrough. <ins> shows added content with an underline. The datetime attribute records when the change was made.

notes: Use <strong> and <em> for semantic meaning (importance and emphasis). Use <b> and <i> for visual styling without semantic meaning.
Screen readers announce <strong> text with extra emphasis. This is why choosing the right tag matters for accessibility.
The <mark> element is ideal for highlighting search terms or important passages. Its default yellow background can be changed with CSS.
The <del> and <ins> elements accept datetime and cite attributes for tracking when and why changes were made.
commonPitfalls: Using <b> and <i> when <strong> and <em> are more appropriate. If the text is genuinely important or emphasized, use the semantic versions.
Overusing <strong> or <em> so that nothing stands out. If everything is bold or italic, nothing is emphasized.
Using <del> or strikethrough purely for visual decoration. The <del> element tells screen readers the text has been deleted, which may confuse users if it is not actually removed content.

exercises.description: Create a product card with: a paragraph with the product name 'Ultra Laptop' in bold (<b>), a paragraph showing the old price '$999' wrapped in <del> and new price '$749' wrapped in <ins>, a paragraph with the word 'limited' emphasized using <em>, and a <small> element with the text 'While supplies last.'

$999 $749

This is a limited time offer.

While supplies last.
hints: Use <b> for the product name (visual bold without extra importance)
Use <del> for the old price and <ins> for the new price

exercises.title: Scientific Notation

exercises.starterCode: <!-- Write the paragraph with subscript and superscript -->
exercises.solution: The chemical formula for glucose is C6H12O6 and Einstein's famous equation is E = mc2.
hints: Use <sub> for numbers in chemical formulas that appear below the baseline
Use <sup> for the exponent 2 in mc2

content.explanation:
Lists are one of the most commonly used HTML structures. They organize related items into a clear, scannable format. HTML provides three types of lists: unordered lists (<ul>) for items where order does not matter, ordered lists (<ol>) for sequential or ranked items, and definition lists (<dl>) for term-description pairs. Lists are block-level elements and are used everywhere - navigation menus, feature lists, step-by-step instructions, FAQs, and more.

Unordered lists use the <ul> element as a container and <li> (list item) elements for each item. By default, browsers render unordered lists with bullet points (disc markers). The <ul> element should only contain <li> elements as direct children - never put bare text or other elements directly inside <ul>. However, each <li> can contain any HTML content including paragraphs, links, images, and even other lists.

Ordered lists use the <ol> element and also contain <li> items. By default, items are numbered starting from 1. The <ol> element has useful attributes: type controls the numbering style (1 for numbers, A for uppercase letters, a for lowercase, I for Roman numerals, i for lowercase Roman), start sets the starting number, and reversed reverses the counting order. Individual <li> elements can use the value attribute to set a specific number, which also resets the count for subsequent items.

Definition lists use <dl> as the container, <dt> for the term being defined, and <dd> for the definition or description. Each <dt> can have one or more <dd> elements, and a <dl> can have multiple <dt>/<dd> pairs. Definition lists are semantically perfect for glossaries, FAQ pages, metadata displays (like 'Author: Jane Doe'), and any content with a label-value structure. They are underused despite being very useful.

Lists can be nested to create hierarchical structures. You nest a list by placing a new <ul> or <ol> inside an <li> element (not directly inside the parent <ul> or <ol>). Browsers automatically change the bullet style for nested unordered lists (disc, circle, square) and adjust indentation. Nested lists are commonly used for multi-level navigation menus, outlines, and category trees. You can mix list types - an <ol> inside a <ul> or vice versa - to create flexible hierarchies.

codeExamples.title: Unordered and Ordered Lists
codeExamples.code:
Shopping List

  • Milk
  • Bread
  • Eggs
  • Butter

Setup Instructions

  1. Download the installer
  2. Run the setup wizard
  3. Accept the license agreement
  4. Choose installation directory
  5. Click Install
codeExamples.output: A bulleted shopping list and a numbered set of instructions.
codeExamples.explanation: Use <ul> when the order of items does not matter (ingredients, features). Use <ol> when order is significant (steps, rankings).

codeExamples.title: Ordered List Attributes
codeExamples.code:
1. Fifth item
  2. Sixth item
  3. Seventh item
  1. Bronze medal
  2. Silver medal
  3. Gold medal
  1. Option A
  2. Option B
  3. Option C
  1. Chapter I
  2. Chapter II
  3. Chapter III
codeExamples.output: Lists with custom start numbers, reversed counting, letter numbering, and Roman numeral numbering.
codeExamples.explanation: The <ol> element supports start (starting number), reversed (count down), and type (1, A, a, I, i) attributes for flexible numbering.

codeExamples.title: Definition Lists
codeExamples.code:
Web Development Terms

HTML
HyperText Markup Language. The standard language for creating web pages.
CSS
Cascading Style Sheets. Controls the visual presentation of HTML elements.
JavaScript
A programming language that adds interactivity to web pages.
Can also be used server-side with Node.js.
Author
Jane Doe
Published
March 11, 2026
Category
Web Development
codeExamples.output: A glossary of web terms and a metadata display with label-value pairs.
codeExamples.explanation: Definition lists (<dl>) are perfect for term-description pairs. A term (<dt>) can have multiple descriptions (<dd>). They are ideal for glossaries, FAQs, and metadata.

codeExamples.title: Nested Lists
codeExamples.code:
• Frontend
    • HTML
    • CSS
      • Flexbox
      • Grid
    • JavaScript
  • Backend
    • Node.js
    • Python
    • Go
  1. Gather materials:
    • Flour
    • Sugar
    • Eggs
  2. Mix ingredients together
  3. Bake at 350F for 25 minutes
codeExamples.output: A hierarchical technology tree and recipe steps with ingredient sub-lists.
codeExamples.explanation: Nest lists by placing a <ul> or <ol> inside an <li> element. Browsers automatically adjust bullet styles and indentation for nested levels. You can mix ordered and unordered lists.

notes: Only <li> elements should be direct children of <ul> and <ol>. Other content goes inside the <li> tags.
Nested lists must be placed inside an <li> element, not directly inside the parent <ul> or <ol>.
Definition lists (<dl>) are underused but perfect for glossaries, FAQs, and any key-value style content.
Navigation menus are typically built with <ul> lists containing <a> links inside <li> elements, wrapped in a <nav>.
commonPitfalls: Placing content directly inside <ul> or <ol> without wrapping it in <li>. Only <li> elements are valid direct children of list containers.
Nesting a <ul> or <ol> directly inside another <ul> or <ol> instead of inside an <li>. The nested list must be a child of a list item.
Using lists purely for indentation or visual formatting. Lists carry semantic meaning and should represent actual lists of related items.

content.explanation:
HTML provides dedicated elements for quoting content from other sources and displaying preformatted text like code snippets. Using these elements correctly is important for both semantic accuracy and proper visual presentation. They tell browsers and assistive technologies that certain content is a quotation or should preserve its exact formatting. The
element is used for long quotations that are typically displayed as an indented block. It should be used when quoting a substantial passage from another source - a paragraph or more. The cite attribute can specify the URL of the source. Inside a
, you can use any block-level elements like

,

, etc. For short inline quotations within a sentence, use the element instead, which automatically adds quotation marks around the content in most browsers. The element (not to be confused with the cite attribute) is used to reference the title of a creative work - a book, article, movie, song, or similar. It renders in italics by default. You can combine
with to provide proper attribution: the
contains the quoted text, and a element references the source. Note that should contain the title of the work, not the author's name (though this convention is debated). The 
 element preserves all whitespace and line breaks exactly as they appear in the HTML source code. Normal HTML collapses whitespace, but 
 disables this behavior. It uses a monospace font by default. The 
 element is essential for displaying code snippets, ASCII art, or any text where exact formatting matters. It is almost always paired with the  element when showing programming code: 
your code here
.

Several related elements help display computer-related content semantically. The  element marks inline code or code snippets - variable names, function calls, file names, etc. The  element represents keyboard input, showing users what keys to press. The  element represents sample output from a computer program. These elements all render in a monospace font by default but carry different semantic meanings. Using them correctly helps screen readers convey the nature of the content - for example, a screen reader can announce  content as keyboard input.

codeExamples.title: Blockquotes and Citations
codeExamples.code:
The power of the Web is in its universality. Access by everyone regardless of disability is an essential aspect.

-- Tim Berners-Lee, W3C Director

As the saying goes, practice makes perfect, which is why exercises are so important in learning HTML.

In their review, the author noted:

This book changed the way I think about web design.
codeExamples.output: An indented block quotation with attribution, an inline quote with quotation marks, and a nested blockquote.
codeExamples.explanation: Use <blockquote> for long quotes from external sources and <q> for short inline quotes. The <cite> element references the title or source of the work. The cite attribute on blockquote holds the source URL.

codeExamples.title: Preformatted Text and Code
codeExamples.code:
JavaScript Example

function greet(name) {
    if (!name) {
        return "Hello, stranger!";
    }
    return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}

HTML Example

<div class="container">
  <h1>Title</h1>
  <p>Content goes here.</p>
</div>
  /\_/\
 ( o.o )
  > ^ <
 /|   |\
(_|   |_)
codeExamples.output: A JavaScript code block and an HTML code block with proper formatting, and a cat ASCII art drawing.
codeExamples.explanation: The <pre> element preserves whitespace and line breaks. When showing HTML code, use < for < and > for > so they are displayed literally instead of being parsed as tags.

codeExamples.title: Inline Code, Keyboard, and Sample Output
codeExamples.code:
Use the document.getElementById() method to select elements by their ID.

Install the package by running npm install express in your terminal.

Press Ctrl + C to copy text.

Press Ctrl + Shift + I to open developer tools.

The program will display: Build completed successfully.

Running node --version outputs: v20.11.0
codeExamples.output: Paragraphs with inline code, keyboard shortcuts, and program output, each styled in monospace.
codeExamples.explanation: Use <code> for code references, <kbd> for keyboard keys the user should press, and <samp> for program output. All three render in monospace but carry different semantic meanings.

codeExamples.title: Combining Quotes and Code
codeExamples.code:
The <pre> element represents preformatted text which is to be presented exactly as written in the HTML file.

-- MDN Web Docs

How do I center a div?

Use Flexbox on the parent:

.parent {
    display: flex;
    justify-content: center;
    align-items: center;
}
codeExamples.output: A technical quote referencing the pre element, and an FAQ answer with a CSS code block.
codeExamples.explanation: HTML elements can be combined freely. Here a blockquote contains inline <code>, and a definition list contains a <pre><code> block for a technical answer.

notes: Use <blockquote> for long quotations from external sources. Use <q> for short inline quotations.
The <cite> element is for titles of works (books, articles, films), not for author names.
Always pair <pre> with <code> when displaying programming code. Use <pre> alone for other preformatted text like ASCII art.
When displaying HTML code inside <pre><code>, use < and > for angle brackets so they are not parsed as actual tags.
commonPitfalls: Using <blockquote> for indentation rather than actual quotations. If you need visual indentation, use CSS margin-left instead.
Forgetting to escape HTML entities inside <pre><code> blocks. Writing <div> inside a code example will be parsed as an actual div element rather than displayed as text.
Confusing the <cite> element with the cite attribute. The <cite> element wraps a work title; the cite attribute on <blockquote> holds the source URL.

The <q> element is used inline within a paragraph for short quotes

Apply this by adding it to your styles.css file.
hints:
Wrap the code block in 
...
